title: Beeswax jar candle with lavender
subtitle: A 200 ml container candle in beeswax with lavender essential oil
excerpt: A container candle made from 100% beeswax in a 200 ml glass jar, scented with lavender essential oil at 4% of wax weight. Beeswax has a higher melt point than soy; it pours at 70-75°C and burns slower and longer.
sourceNotes: Bramble Berry beeswax candle guide (brambleberry.com, accessed May 2026): pour temperature and wick sizing differences from soy. CandleScience beeswax notes (candlescience.com, accessed May 2026): fragrance load limits for beeswax.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 200 ml jar candle (~35-40 burn hours)
recipe.batchNotes: Four jars from 600 g beeswax and 24 g lavender EO.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Beeswax candles benefit from at least 2 days of cure time before the first burn. The scent throw, which is naturally quieter than a soy candle, develops further over a week.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Effectively indefinite unlit. Beeswax is the most stable candle wax. The lavender scent fades after 2 years in an unlit candle. Burn within 2 years for the best result.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first burn of a container candle sets the burn pattern. For a beeswax jar candle, allow the melt pool to reach the jar edge before extinguishing; this typically takes 4-5 hours for a 7 cm jar, longer than soy because beeswax has a higher melt point.
The wax temperature at the moment of pouring. Beeswax pours at 70-75°C, 10-15°C higher than container soy wax. Pouring too cool causes the wax to pull away from the jar walls as it contracts.

The temperature at which a wax transitions from solid to liquid. Beeswax melts at 62-65°C versus 50°C for container soy. The higher melt point is why beeswax candles burn longer and tolerate warmer rooms without sagging.

content.text: Beeswax is the oldest candle material in continuous use. Its
melt point

of 62-65°C means the finished candle is harder and slower-burning than a soy equivalent, which suits a jar format where a slow, even burn pool matters more than a quick melt. The natural honey note of beeswax softens the lavender essential oil, producing a warmer effect than lavender in a neutral wax.
Beeswax holds less fragrance than soy; keep the essential oil load at 3-4% rather than the 6-8% common in soy recipes. Higher loads cause the oil to seep out of the wax as the candle cools.

Melt beeswax over a simmering-water bath and keep the heat moderate. Beeswax has a flash point around 250°C; it is not fire-prone at working temperatures, but never leave a wax-filled pitcher on direct heat. Keep a fire blanket nearby.
What you need

items.prepNote: Cosmetic-grade beeswax pellets or pastilles. A 200 ml jar takes approximately 150-160 g at a standard fill. Beeswax is denser than soy.
4% of wax weight. Add at 70°C as the pitcher nears the pour temperature. Lavandula angustifolia.
CD-14 or ECO-10 for beeswax in a 7 cm jar; beeswax needs a larger wick than soy at the same jar diameter because of its higher melt point.

content.text: Method
Set the wick
Secure the wick tab to the centre of the clean jar base. Thread the wick through the centring bar, pulling it upright.
Melt the beeswax

Put the beeswax pellets in the candle pouring pitcher and set over a saucepan of simmering water. Stir occasionally. Beeswax melts at 62-65°C, which takes longer than soy. Heat to 75°C on the probe thermometer.
Add essential oil and pour
At 70-75°C, stir in the lavender essential oil. Pour slowly into the prepared jar at this
pour temperature
; beeswax poured too cool pulls away from the jar walls and leaves air pockets. Fill to 1 cm below the rim.
Cure and first burn

Allow 2 days undisturbed for the beeswax to cure fully. Beeswax contracts more than soy and a small surface dimple around the wick is normal; top up with a small amount of warm wax if needed. Trim the wick to 5 mm. For the
memory burn
, allow the melt pool to reach the jar edge before extinguishing. This takes 4-5 hours for beeswax in a 7 cm jar, longer than the 3-4 hours for soy in the same jar.
Storage and shelf life

Keep in a cool, dark place. Beeswax bloom (a white haze on the surface) is normal and does not indicate spoilage; it is a natural migration of the wax's lighter components. Wipe off with a soft cloth or warm the surface briefly. The candle is effectively indefinite unlit; the lavender scent fades after 2 years.
